name: Flow

aliases.name: Flow
aliases.comment:
A description of a computation that can read and write state `s` and
eventually produces a value of type `a`.

`Flow` is a free monad: values of this type are data structures that the
runtime interprets, rather than functions that run immediately. This makes
it possible to batch, sequence, and transform computations before any
side-effects take place.

aliases.name: Program
aliases.comment:
The concrete Elm `Platform.Program` type produced by `Flow.element`,
`Flow.document`, and `Flow.application`.
aliases.type: Platform.Program flags ( s, Flow.Program.Model (Flow.Flow s a) ) (Flow.Program.RuntimeMsg (Flow.Flow s a))

values.name: andThen
values.comment:
Chain two Flow computations. The second receives the value produced by the
first.

    Flow.get
        |> Flow.andThen (\m -> Flow.pure m.count)
values.type: (a -> Flow.Flow s b) -> Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: ap
values.comment:
Apply a function wrapped in Flow to a value wrapped in Flow.

    Flow.pure (\n -> n + 1)
        |> Flow.ap (Flow.pure 41)
    -- produces 42
values.type: Flow.Flow s (a -> b) -> Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: application
values.comment:
Like `document` but also handles URL changes, for single-page applications.

    main =
        Flow.application
            { init = \_ url key -> ( initialModel key, loadPage url )
            , view = view
            , subscriptions = \_ -> Sub.none
            , onUrlRequest = handleUrlRequest
            , onUrlChange = handleUrlChange
            }
values.type: { init : flags -> Url.Url -> Browser.Navigation.Key -> ( s, Flow.Flow s a ), view : s -> Browser.Document (Flow.Flow s a), subscriptions : s -> Platform.Sub.Sub (Flow.Flow s a), onUrlRequest : Browser.UrlRequest -> Flow.Flow s a, onUrlChange : Url.Url -> Flow.Flow s a } -> Flow.Program flags s a

values.name: assertCondition
values.comment:
Continue only if the predicate holds for the produced value, terminate
with `none` otherwise.

    Flow.get
        |> Flow.assertCondition (\m -> m.count > 0)
        |> Flow.andThen doSomethingWithPositiveCount
values.type: (a -> Basics.Bool) -> Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: assertJust
values.comment:
Continue only if the value is `Just`, terminate with `none` otherwise.

    fetchUser userId
        |> Flow.assertJust
        |> Flow.andThen renderUser
values.type: Flow.Flow s (Maybe.Maybe a) -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: assertOk
values.comment:
Continue only if the result is `Ok`, silently terminate on `Err`.

    decodeResponse body
        |> Flow.assertOk
        |> Flow.andThen handleData
values.type: Flow.Flow s (Result.Result e a) -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: async
values.comment:
Fire a Flow as a background branch and immediately continue with `()`.
The background branch runs concurrently; its state writes are interleaved
with the rest of the batch.

    Flow.async (longRunningSync model)
        |> Flow.seq nextStep
values.type: Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: attemptTask
values.comment:
Fire a `Task` for its side-effects and ignore the result (both `Ok` and
`Err` are discarded). Useful for fire-and-forget operations such as writing
to `localStorage` via a port task.

    Flow.attemptTask (Ports.saveToLocalStorage key value)
values.type: Task.Task e a -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: await
values.comment:
Suspend this Flow pipeline, open a Channel, and resume when a value arrives.

    type alias Model =
        { latestMessage : String }

    listenOnce : Flow Model ()
    listenOnce =
        Flow.await myChannel
            |> Flow.andThen (\msg -> Flow.modify (\m -> { m | latestMessage = msg }))
values.type: Flow.Channel.Channel s a -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: batch
values.comment:
Run several independent Flow branches concurrently, one per list element.
Each branch shares the same initial state snapshot; state writes from earlier
branches are visible to later ones (left-to-right evaluation order).

    Flow.batch [ 1, 2, 3 ]
        |> Flow.map (\n -> n * 2)
    -- forks into three branches producing 2, 4, 6
values.type: List.List a -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: batchM
values.comment:
Run a list of Flow computations as concurrent branches. Each branch runs
independently; the continuation (via `andThen`) is invoked once per branch
with that branch's individual result.

Use `mapM` instead if you want the results collected into a `List`.

    -- fire three notifications in parallel
    Flow.batchM [ notifyAlice, notifyBob, notifyCarol ]

    -- continuation runs once per branch, not once with a List
    Flow.batchM [ fetchUserA, fetchUserB ]
        |> Flow.andThen (\user -> renderUser user)
values.type: List.List (Flow.Flow s a) -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: bracket_
values.comment:
Acquire a resource, run a computation, then release the resource —
even if the computation terminates early with `none`.

    Flow.bracket_
        (Flow.modify (\m -> { m | loading = True }))
        (Flow.modify (\m -> { m | loading = False }))
        fetchData
values.type: Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s b -> Flow.Flow s c -> Flow.Flow s c

values.name: compose
values.comment:
Compose two Kleisli arrows (functions returning Flow).

    handleResponse : Response -> Flow Model ()
    handleResponse =
        Flow.compose persistToDisk fetchData
values.type: (b -> Flow.Flow m c) -> (a -> Flow.Flow m b) -> a -> Flow.Flow m c

values.name: document
values.comment:
Like `element` but the view function returns a full `Document`, allowing
you to control the page `<title>`.

    main =
        Flow.document
            { init = \_ -> ( initialModel, loadData )
            , view = view -- returns { title = "…", body = […] }
            , subscriptions = \_ -> Sub.none
            }
values.type: { init : flags -> ( s, Flow.Flow s a ), view : s -> Browser.Document (Flow.Flow s a), subscriptions : s -> Platform.Sub.Sub (Flow.Flow s a) } -> Flow.Program flags s a

values.name: element
values.comment:
Build a standard `Browser.element` program whose messages are `Flow`
computations.
values.type: { init : flags -> ( s, Flow.Flow s a ), view : s -> Html.Html (Flow.Flow s a), subscriptions : s -> Platform.Sub.Sub (Flow.Flow s a) } -> Flow.Program flags s a

values.name: flap
values.comment:
Flipped `ap` — apply a value to a function, both wrapped in Flow.

    Flow.pure 41
        |> Flow.flap (Flow.pure (\n -> n + 1))
    -- produces 42
values.type: Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s (a -> b) -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: forAll
values.comment:
Read a value through an optic, terminating with `none` if the optic does
not match.

    Flow.forAll MyModel.selectedItem
        (\item -> renderItem item)
values.type: Accessors.An_Optic pr ls s a -> (a -> Flow.Flow s b) -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: forceRendering
values.comment:
Wrap a Flow so that every state write inside it is followed by a render
cycle, giving the browser a chance to repaint between steps.

Useful when you need the UI to visually reflect intermediate states during a
multi-step computation rather than only showing the final result.

    -- the UI repaints after each step, not just at the end
    Flow.forceRendering <|
        Flow.mapM [ setStepOne, setStepTwo, setStepThree ]
values.type: Flow.Flow a b -> Flow.Flow a b

values.name: fromMaybe
values.comment:
Unwrap a `Maybe` and feed the inner value into a continuation, or
terminate with `none` if the `Maybe` is `Nothing`.

    Flow.fromMaybe model.selectedId
        (\id -> fetchItem id |> Flow.andThen renderItem)
values.type: Maybe.Maybe a -> (a -> Flow.Flow s b) -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: get
values.comment:
Read the current model.

    Flow.get
        |> Flow.andThen (\model -> Flow.pure model.count)
values.type: Flow.Flow s s

values.name: getAll
values.comment:
Read all values targeted by an optic (useful for traversals).

    Flow.getAll MyModel.allItems
        (\items -> Flow.pure (List.length items))
values.type: Accessors.An_Optic pr ls s a -> (List.List a -> Flow.Flow s b) -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: join
values.comment:
Flatten a nested `Flow s (Flow s a)` into `Flow s a`.

    Flow.pure (Flow.pure 42)
        |> Flow.join
    -- same as Flow.pure 42
values.type: Flow.Flow s (Flow.Flow s a) -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: lift
values.comment:
Lift an Elm `Cmd` into Flow. The command is executed and its result
becomes the value produced by this Flow step.

    Flow.lift (Http.get { url = "/ping", expect = Http.expectString identity })
        |> Flow.andThen handleResponse
values.type: Platform.Cmd.Cmd a -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: liftUpdate
values.comment:
Lift a classic TEA `update`-style function into Flow. Reads the current
model, applies the function, writes the new model, and emits any commands.

    Flow.liftUpdate (MyOldModule.update msg)
values.type: (s -> ( s, Platform.Cmd.Cmd a )) -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: locking
values.comment:
Run a computation only if a `Bool` lens is currently `False`, and hold
it at `True` for the duration. Subsequent calls while the lock is held are
silently dropped.

    Flow.locking MyModel.isSaving saveRecord
values.type: Accessors.A_Lens pr s Basics.Bool -> Flow.Flow s () -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: map
values.comment:
Transform the value produced by a Flow without changing state.

    Flow.pure 3
        |> Flow.map (\n -> n * 2)
    -- produces 6
values.type: (a -> b) -> Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: mapM
values.comment:
Sequence a list of Flow computations, collecting all results.

Equivalent to `traverse identity`.

    Flow.mapM [ step1, step2, step3 ]
        |> Flow.andThen finalize
values.type: List.List (Flow.Flow s a) -> Flow.Flow s (List.List a)

values.name: modify
values.comment:
Apply a function to transform the model.

    Flow.modify (\m -> { m | count = m.count + 1 })
values.type: (s -> s) -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: none
values.comment:
The empty computation — terminates the current branch without producing a
value or changing state. Use this as the natural end-point of a pipeline.

    Flow.get
        |> Flow.andThen
            (\m ->
                if m.ready then
                    doWork

                else
                    Flow.none
            )
values.type: Flow.Flow s a

values.name: over
values.comment:
Modify the value(s) targeted by an optic.

    Flow.over MyModel.count (\n -> n + 1)
values.type: Accessors.An_Optic pr ls s a -> (a -> a) -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: pure
values.comment:
Lift a plain value into Flow without performing any effects.

    Flow.pure 42
        |> Flow.map (\n -> n + 1)
    -- produces 43, state unchanged
values.type: a -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: replace
values.comment:
Re-interpret a `Flow a x` inside a larger context `Flow b x` by providing
a getter (`Flow b a`) and a setter (`a -> Flow b ()`).

This is the primitive that `via` and the other optics helpers are built on.
Use `via` in normal code; reach for `replace` only when you need a custom
traversal that optics cannot express.
values.type: Flow.Flow b a -> (a -> Flow.Flow b ()) -> Flow.Flow a x -> Flow.Flow b x

values.name: return
values.comment:
Run a Flow for its effects, then produce a fixed value `a` regardless of
what the flow returned.

    saveRecord
        |> Flow.return "saved"
values.type: a -> Flow.Flow s b -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: seq
values.comment:
Run the piped Flow for its effects, discard its result, then run the
argument Flow and return its result.

    saveRecord
        |> Flow.seq (Flow.pure "saved!")
    -- runs saveRecord, then produces "saved!"
values.type: Flow.Flow s b -> Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: set
values.comment:
Replace the model entirely.

    Flow.set { count = 0 }
values.type: s -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: setAll
values.comment:
Set the value(s) targeted by an optic.

    Flow.setAll MyModel.status Loading
values.type: Accessors.An_Optic pr ls s a -> a -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: setting
values.comment:
Set a `Bool` field to `True` for the duration of a computation, then
restore it to `False`. Useful for loading/busy flags.

    Flow.setting MyModel.isLoading fetchData
values.type: Accessors.An_Optic pr ls s Basics.Bool -> Flow.Flow s a -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: subscribe
values.comment:
Open a channel and run the given handler for every value it produces,
indefinitely.

    listenForever : Flow Model ()
    listenForever =
        Flow.subscribe
            (\msg -> Flow.modify (\m -> { m | latestMessage = msg }))
            myChannel
values.type: (a -> Flow.Flow s ()) -> Flow.Channel.Channel s a -> Flow.Flow s a

values.name: traverse
values.comment:
Map over a list with a function that returns Flow, collecting the results
in order.

    Flow.traverse fetchUser userIds
        |> Flow.andThen renderAll
values.type: (a -> Flow.Flow s b) -> List.List a -> Flow.Flow s (List.List b)

values.name: try
values.comment:
Read a value through an optic. Produces `Just a` if the optic matches,
`Nothing` if it does not (e.g. a `Prism` pointing at the wrong variant).

    Flow.try MyModel.selectedItem
        (\maybeItem ->
            case maybeItem of
                Just item ->
                    renderItem item

                Nothing ->
                    showPlaceholder
        )
values.type: Accessors.An_Optic pr ls s a -> (Maybe.Maybe a -> Flow.Flow s b) -> Flow.Flow s b

values.name: via
values.comment:
Run a `Flow a x` in the context of a larger model `s`, using an optic to
zoom in and out. State reads and writes inside the sub-flow are applied through
the optic.

    Flow.via MyModel.editingRecord
        (Flow.modify (\r -> { r | name = newName }))
values.type: Accessors.An_Optic pr ls s a -> Flow.Flow a x -> Flow.Flow s x

values.name: when
values.comment:
Run a Flow only when the condition is `True`, otherwise produce `()`.

    Flow.when model.isLoggedIn savePreferences
values.type: Basics.Bool -> Flow.Flow s () -> Flow.Flow s ()

values.name: yield
values.comment:
Yield a value back to the Elm runtime, allowing the browser to repaint,
then continue with the value. Equivalent to `Process.sleep 0`.

    -- ensure the UI reflects intermediate state before a heavy computation
    Flow.set loadingModel
        |> Flow.seq (Flow.yield ())
        |> Flow.seq heavyComputation
values.type: a -> Flow.Flow s a

name: Flow.Channel

unions.name: Channel
unions.comment:
An opaque handle describing how to connect an external event source to a
`Flow` pipeline. Build one with `connect` or `join`, then pass it to
`Flow.await` or `Flow.subscribe`.

values.name: accept
values.comment:
Subscribe to a channel and run the handler for every incoming event,
indefinitely. The subscription runs for the lifetime of the program with
no built-in way to stop it.

    listenForever : Flow Model ()
    listenForever =
        Flow.Channel.accept
            (\msg -> Flow.modify (\m -> { m | lastMessage = msg }))
            myChannel
values.type: (a -> Flow.Internal.Flow s ()) -> Flow.Channel.Channel s a -> Flow.Internal.Flow s a

values.name: acceptOne
values.comment:
Wait for the next single event from a channel and return it. The channel
is closed after the first event arrives.

    -- read one message then continue
    Flow.Channel.acceptOne myChannel
        |> Flow.andThen (\msg -> Flow.modify (\m -> { m | lastMessage = msg }))
values.type: Flow.Channel.Channel s a -> Flow.Internal.Flow s a

values.name: acceptUntil
values.comment:
Wait for events from a channel, discarding each one until the predicate
returns `True`. Returns the first event that satisfies the predicate and
then closes the channel.

    -- wait until the server sends "done"
    Flow.Channel.acceptUntil (\msg -> msg == "done") myChannel
values.type: (a -> Basics.Bool) -> Flow.Channel.Channel s a -> Flow.Internal.Flow s a

values.name: connect
values.comment:
Build a channel from a subscription source and a command that opens or
attaches it. The command is fired once when the channel is first awaited.

    -- Port declarations:
    --   port onMessage : (String -> msg) -> Sub msg
    --   port subscribe : String -> Cmd msg

    myChannel : Channel Model String
    myChannel =
        Flow.Channel.connect
            (\callback -> Ports.onMessage callback)
            (\_ -> Ports.subscribe "myTopic")
values.type: ((a -> Maybe.Maybe (Flow.Internal.Flow s a)) -> Platform.Sub.Sub (Maybe.Maybe (Flow.Internal.Flow s a))) -> (Flow.Channel.ChannelKey -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd (Flow.Internal.Flow s a)) -> Flow.Channel.Channel s a

values.name: filter
values.comment:
Filter events for a channel. Only events for which the predicate returns
`True` are forwarded; others are silently dropped.

The predicate receives the internal channel key and the event payload.
In most cases you only need the payload:

    Flow.Channel.filter (\_ msg -> msg /= "") myChannel
values.type: (Flow.Channel.ChannelKey -> a -> Basics.Bool) -> Flow.Channel.Channel s a -> Flow.Channel.Channel s a

values.name: join
values.comment:
Build a channel from a subscription source when no startup command is needed.

    -- Port declaration:
    --   port onKeyDown : (String -> msg) -> Sub msg

    keyChannel : Channel Model String
    keyChannel =
        Flow.Channel.join (\callback -> Ports.onKeyDown callback)
values.type: ((a -> Maybe.Maybe (Flow.Internal.Flow s a)) -> Platform.Sub.Sub (Maybe.Maybe (Flow.Internal.Flow s a))) -> Flow.Channel.Channel s a
